content:
When not to use llama.cpp ?

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by DAVIS VARGAS on UnsplashFor many developers, running a local LLM starts with one recommendation:
“Just use llama.cpp.”
At this point, llama.cpp has become the default answer for almost every local AI discussion. Need to run a model on your laptop? Use llama.cpp. Need offline AI? Use llama.cpp. Need a chatbot? Use llama.cpp.
The problem is that many people are using llama.cpp simply because everyone else does.
After spending time experimenting with local LLMs, I realized that llama.cpp is excellent for specific use cases, but often the wrong choice for many others.

What llama.cpp Gets Right
Before criticizing it, let’s acknowledge what made llama.cpp so popular. It can run models on:

• CPUs

• Consumer GPUs

• Macs

• Raspberry Pis

• Edge devices
It supports GGUF quantization, has a huge community, and makes local inference accessible to almost anyone. Without llama.cpp, local AI would probably not be where it is today.
But popularity doesn’t automatically make it the best option.

Don’t Use llama.cpp If You Have a Powerful GPU
One of the strangest trends in the local AI community is watching people buy expensive GPUs and then immediately spend hours trying to run heavily quantized models. A developer buys an RTX 4090.
Then they:

• Download a Q4 model

• Compress everything possible

• Sacrifice quality

• Tune settings endlessly
All to save memory they already have.
If you own a high-end GPU, frameworks like vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and SGLang are often better suited for extracting maximum performance from that hardware.
llama.cpp was designed to make models fit on limited hardware.
If hardware is no longer your limitation, its advantages become less compelling.

Don’t Use llama.cpp for Production AI Services
Many projects begin as simple experiments. A single user sends prompts. Everything works. Then the project grows. Suddenly you need:

• Concurrent users

• Request batching

• Monitoring

• Metrics

• Higher throughput
This is where llama.cpp starts showing its limits. Dedicated inference servers were built specifically for these scenarios.
Using llama.cpp as a production backend can feel like using a bicycle to deliver packages across an entire city. It works, but there are better tools for the job.

Don’t Use llama.cpp If You Just Want AI to Work
Many newcomers underestimate how much local AI involves infrastructure rather than AI. A typical workflow might involve:

• Finding a model

• Downloading GGUF files

• Choosing quantization levels

• Testing memory usage

• Adjusting context settings

• Benchmarking performance
Before long, you’re spending more time tuning models than actually using them. For developers who simply want an AI assistant, cloud APIs often provide a much faster path from idea to execution.
The money saved by running locally can easily be outweighed by the time spent maintaining everything.

Don’t Use llama.cpp for Fine-Tuning
A surprisingly common misconception is that llama.cpp is a complete LLM development platform. It isn’t. Its primary focus is inference.
If your goal is training or fine-tuning models, frameworks like Hugging Face Transformers, Axolotl, or Unsloth provide a much smoother experience.
Trying to build an entire training workflow around llama.cpp is like buying a Formula 1 steering wheel and expecting it to be the entire car.

Don’t Use llama.cpp If You Always Want the Latest Models
Every major model release follows a familiar pattern. The model launches. Developers get excited. Then people start asking:
“Where is the GGUF version?”
“Has it been quantized yet?”
“Does llama.cpp support it?”
Sometimes support arrives quickly. Sometimes it doesn’t.
If you prefer using models immediately after release, model-native frameworks usually get support first. The local ecosystem often spends days or weeks catching up.

The Real Problem: We’re Optimizing the Wrong Thing
The biggest issue isn’t llama.cpp itself. It’s how people use it. Many developers spend days squeezing giant models into tiny amounts of RAM.
They obsess over quantization settings.
They compare token generation speeds.
They benchmark endlessly.
Meanwhile, they could have been building products, applications, or workflows that actually create value. Optimization becomes the hobby. The model becomes secondary.

When You Should Absolutely Use llama.cpp
Despite everything above, llama.cpp remains one of the most important projects in AI. Use it when you need:

• Offline inference

• Privacy-focused deployments

• CPU-only execution

• Edge devices

• Older hardware

• Portable local AI setups
These are exactly the problems llama.cpp was designed to solve.

Final Thoughts
llama.cpp is not overrated.
It’s over-recommended.
Too many people start with it before asking what problem they’re trying to solve. If your goal is running AI on limited hardware, llama.cpp is one of the best tools available.
But if you have powerful GPUs, need production-scale serving, want the latest model support, or simply want AI to work with minimal effort, there may be better options.
The best local AI setup isn’t the one with the most optimizations. It’s the one that lets you stop optimizing and start building.
